Interpretation: Fraction of total student demand successfully served by the ecosystem
Data Required: Demand, served meals
Contribution to EVI: Direct component of operational efficiency and mission achievement
Campus Food Example: If demand is 500 meals and 450 are served, fulfillment is 0.90

Interpretation: Fraction of demand that could not be served
Data Required: Demand, served meals
Contribution to EVI: Negative indicator for fulfillment and satisfaction
Campus Food Example: High shortage during exam week indicates insufficient production capacity

Interpretation: Average time students wait before receiving service
Data Required: Queue length, service rate, service capacity
Contribution to EVI: Affects satisfaction, convenience, and mission achievement
Campus Food Example: Long lunch queues reduce student satisfaction

Interpretation: Degree to which vendor production capacity is used
Data Required: Vendor output, vendor capacity
Contribution to EVI: Supports vendor viability and operational efficiency
Campus Food Example: Vendor operating at 85% capacity may be healthy; 20% may indicate underuse

Interpretation: Degree to which stakeholder mission intensities are aligned in time
Normalization Method: Convert dispersion of MIG values to [0,1]
Data Required: Mission intensity values for all stakeholders
Campus Food Example: High when suppliers, vendors, and delivery agents peak before lunch demand

Interpretation: Total food waste generated from overproduction and leftovers
Data Required: Production quantity, demand served by vendor, leftover rate
Contribution to EVI: Negative component of sustainability and waste reduction
Campus Food Example: Overproduction by vendors increases raw food waste

Interpretation: Higher score means lower waste relative to production
Data Required: Raw waste, total production
Campus Food Example: MOEE should increase this score by coordinating production with demand

Interpretation: Composite environmental and resource-sustainability performance
Data Required: Waste score, emission proxy, reuse rate, resource efficiency
Campus Food Example: Plastic ban may improve sustainability but affect cost

Interpretation: Proxy for emissions from food production, delivery, and waste
Data Required: Production quantity, delivery distance, waste, emission coefficients
Campus Food Example: Long delivery routes and high waste increase carbon proxy

Interpretation: Financial viability of vendor v
Data Required: Revenue, cost, subsidy, penalty
Campus Food Example: Inflation reduces vendor profit unless price or subsidy adjusts

Interpretation: Measures whether vendors remain financially sustainable
Normalization Method: Average normalized vendor profit and survival indicators
Data Required: Vendor profit, break-even threshold, operating status
Campus Food Example: If vendors operate below break-even, mission sustainability is threatened

Interpretation: Degree to which meals remain financially accessible to students
Data Required: Meal prices, student budget, subsidy
Contribution to EVI: Student welfare and mission achievement component
Campus Food Example: Inflation lowers affordability unless subsidies or menu adjustments are introduced

Interpretation: Weighted average nutritional quality of meals consumed
Data Required: Meals served by vendor, vendor nutrition scores
Contribution to EVI: Student health and mission achievement component
Campus Food Example: Nutrition-first policy should increase average nutrition score

Interpretation: Composite measure of student experience and welfare
Data Required: Affordability, waiting time, nutrition, menu diversity, service quality
Contribution to EVI: Direct component of mission achievement and value
Campus Food Example: Students are more satisfied when meals are affordable, healthy, and fast

Interpretation: Availability of sufficient menu variety
Data Required: Menu count, menu target, vendor operating status
Campus Food Example: Vendor exit reduces menu diversity

Interpretation: Degree to which the ecosystem fulfills its mission-oriented goals
Data Required: Fulfillment, nutrition, affordability, sustainability, vendor viability, satisfaction
Campus Food Example: MOEE succeeds if campus welfare, sustainability, and vendor viability improve together

Interpretation: Severity of stakeholder-specific bottleneck that constrains ecosystem mission
Data Required: Required output, actual output by stakeholder
Contribution to EVI: Negative signal for resilience and mission achievement
Campus Food Example: Supplier bottleneck reduces vendor ability to produce meals

Interpretation: Fraction of MSRS recommendations accepted by stakeholders
Data Required: Issued and accepted recommendations
Contribution to EVI: Indicates adoption quality of PUDAL/MSRS coordination
Campus Food Example: Vendor accepts production recommendation when expected benefit is credible

Interpretation: Observed improvement attributable to accepted recommendations
Normalization Method: Normalize relative improvement to [0,1] if used in EVI
Data Required: Counterfactual baseline, observed outcome, recommendation log
Campus Food Example: Waste falls after vendors accept production recommendations

Interpretation: Relative error between predicted and actual demand
Data Required: Predicted demand, actual demand
Contribution to EVI: Indirectly affects recommendation quality and waste/shortage reduction
Campus Food Example: AI forecasting upgrade should reduce demand forecast error

Interpretation: Improvement in policy performance over time due to PUDAL learning
Normalization Method: Can be averaged over time or normalized by initial EVI
Data Required: EVI time series by policy
Contribution to EVI: Learning quality indicator, not always direct EVI component
Campus Food Example: Adaptive policy improves after observing repeated lunch surges

Interpretation: Ability to maintain performance under disruption
Data Required: Normal performance trajectory, shock performance trajectory
Campus Food Example: MOEE should preserve higher EVI during multi-shock crisis

Interpretation: Time required to recover to acceptable performance after disruption
Data Required: Performance trajectory, shock timing, recovery threshold τ
Campus Food Example: Vendor failure recovery is faster if demand is redistributed quickly

Interpretation: Evaluates policy k across multiple scenarios using average, worst-case, and variance
Normalization Method: Normalize RI across policies for final ranking
Data Required: EVI values for each policy across scenarios
Contribution to EVI: Used for selecting best MSRS policy, not a per-step EVI component
Campus Food Example: Balanced-EVI policy may have the best robustness across disruptions

Interpretation: Degree to which vendor benefits are fairly distributed
Data Required: Vendor profits
Campus Food Example: Recommendation system should not send all demand to one vendor only

Interpretation: Fairness of food access across student groups or locations
Data Required: Access scores by student group, location, or income group
Campus Food Example: Scholarship students should retain affordable access under inflation

Interpretation: Fraction of audited actions satisfying regulatory, safety, fairness, and environmental constraints
Data Required: Audited actions, compliance results
Campus Food Example: Vendors comply with hygiene and labeling rules

Interpretation: Reliability of supplier delivery relative to vendor requirements
Data Required: Required deliveries, on-time deliveries
Campus Food Example: Supply chain disruption lowers supplier reliability
Simulation Placeholder: [to be computed in expanded supply model]

Interpretation: Reliability of delivery service under online and MOEE models
Data Required: Requested deliveries, completed on-time deliveries
Campus Food Example: Extreme weather lowers delivery reliability

Interpretation: Composite ecosystem performance index integrating operational, mission, sustainability, welfare, financial, and resilience dimensions
Normalization Method: Weighted aggregation of normalized metrics; Σω=1
Data Required: All EVI component metrics
Campus Food Example: MOEE is superior if it increases average EVI and improves worst-case EVI under disruptions
